observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the CAO and CTO to regularly reconcile their records, in coordination with the City Assessor’s Office, in order to eliminate the variance, and to accrue the proper amount of receivables at year-end.

We also recommended that Management upgrade the eGAPS – Real Property Tax Management System and Real Property Assessment System to enable the CTO to generate reports on delinquencies / receivables for specific period, and on adjustments made for submission to the CAO for proper recording in the books.
observations.managementAction:
Continuous coordination is undertaken by the City Treasury Office (CTO) and City Accounting Office (CAO) to address the recommendation.

Meetings with the system provider were conducted for the possible upgrade of the electronic Government Application System (eGAPS).
observations.reason: RPT Receivable and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivable in balances has unreconciled balance totaling P202,639,682.05 between the CTO and CAO records as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Office (CHO) to coordinate with the accredited health care institutions to:

Maintain monitoring reports indicating the total number of member patients with billings and amount of claims to PhilHealth; and

Regularly submit all PhilHealth claims for services rendered to member patients with supporting documents to the City Accountant to facilitate the recording of receivables in the Due from GOCCs account.
observations.managementAction: The Out- patient HIV/Aids Treatment (OHAT) facility has started maintain and forwarding reports to CAO for recording in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: However, no reports were forwarded to the CAO for recording in the books of accounts of other accredited HCIs of the City.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:

Commence civil remedies such as distraint of personal property or issuance of warrant of levy on real properties or other applicable actions relative to the dormant receivables pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 7160; and

Conduct regular monitoring of past-due receivables and ensure prompt collections when receivables become due and demandable.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to conduct regular analysis and verification of the Other Receivables account so as to identify the details and collectability of all receivable items which remained dormant in the books.
observations.managementAction:
The CTO coordinated with the City Auditor’s Office for write-off of receivables.

Regular monitoring, analysis and verification of the Other Receivables account were undertaken.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2024, no write-off of dormant accounts, legal actions or civil remedies were initiated by the CTO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

General Services Officer to – 

Properly prepare the RPCI for all types of inventory accounts indicating the complete details on the quantities per card and on hand, and shortage or overage, if any, according to the instructions provided in the MNGAS for LGUs;

Prepare the RPCI using the moving average method pursuant to Section 115 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and

Ensure submission of necessary documents to the CAO such as the RIS and SSMI in order for the CAO to prepare necessary journal entries.

City Accountant and General Services Officer to –

Analyze and identify the discrepancies between the Inventory Schedule and RPCI and take necessary actions to resolve the same; and

Conduct regular reconciliation of the inventory balance account and clear any discrepancies immediately, if any, to ensure fair presentation of the inventory accounts at year-end.
observations.managementAction:
The General Services Office (GSO) committed to comply with the preparation of the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI), containing the details on the quantities per card and on hand, and will adopt the weighted average cost of each item.

Necessary documents were submitted hence resulting in a significant reduction in the inventory variance in CY 2024.

The CAO and GSO identified and adjusted the discrepancies, thus, reduced the inventory variance in CY 2024.

The CAO and GSO reconciled the inventory variance, hence, the significant decrease of the same in CY 2024.
observations.reason: No RPCI was prepared and submitted for Accountable Forms, Plates and Stickers Inventory account as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct:

The Inventory Committee to –

Conduct physical count of all PPEs and eliminate the lump sum amounts in the RPCPPE;

Review PPE items with noted discrepancies in coordination with the CAO, including those reported twice, with different acquisition costs compared to CAO records, and that no longer exist, and inform the City Accountant for proper recording in the books;

Reconcile the RPCPPE with CAO records based on the actual physical count of PPE; and

Make the necessary adjustments identified during reconciliation to arrive at the correct PPE balances.

The General Services Officer to forward the Property Cards to City Auditor’s Office so as to facilitate the verification and reconciliation of the balances with the CAO records.

The City Accountant to –

Coordinate with the Inventory Committee to reconcile the PPE balances in the books/PPE Schedule against the reported balances in the RPCPPE as of December 31, 2024 and adjust any discrepancies noted; and

Make the necessary adjustments in the books relative to the overstatement of P104,000.00 in the Office Equipment account for PPE items which were recorded twice.

City provide detailed explanation on why the Balubad Market located at Bagong Sibol, Balubad, Nangka, Marikina City has been inoperative, as well as information on the upcoming bridge project of the DPWH that was affecting its utilization.
observations.managementAction:
The GSO continuously reconciled with the CAO and submitted the necessary documents to facilitate the recording of existing PPEs.

The GSO is maintaining PCs per category of PPE.

Continuous coordination with CAO is undertaken to reconcile the PPE balances recorded in the books and RPCPPE as of December 31, 2024. The PPE items were identified to be unserviceable, while others were pending verification.

The Office forwarded copies of PCs to the City Auditor’s Office.

The CAO prepared adjustments (JEVs) and coordinated with GSO for reconciliation; items below capitalization threshold adjusted per indicated JEVs.
observations.reason: There were lump sum amounts presented as reconciling items or beginning balances in the RPCPPE totaling P49,649,419.78. PCs were forwarded, however, the same were not maintained for all PPE accounts. The X-ray machine remains non-operational due to an electrical voltage issue.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to post project signboards on the place where the PPAs are located and to submit: The list of all ongoing infrastructure projects and those for implementation for the current year containing the information required under Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; The monitoring report on a monthly basis; and Information to COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboard and/or public notice is already posted on a per project basis.
observations.managementAction: The CEO committed to comply with the recommendation.
observations.reason: No project signboards were installed for ongoing projects per inspection. Not all projects had signboards installed during inspection and the required documents were also not submitted to the City Auditor’s Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed to the recommendations that the: LCCAP Core Team initiate discussions on updating the LCCAP of the City pursuant to DILG MC No. 2014-135; and LCCAP be regularly and completely updated, at least annually to reflect changing social, economic, and environmental conditions and emerging issues in compliance with DILG MC No. 2014-135.
observations.managementAction: The Core Team initiated discussions relative on the updating of LCCAP, per interview with City Environmental Management Office (CEMO).
observations.reason: The LCCAP of the City of Marikina was not updated in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CEMO to: Prepare the M&E Report/AR for LCCAP for CY 2024 and submit the same to the City Auditor’s Office for verification; and Coordinate with the City Planning and Development Officer in the inclusion of CC adaptation and mitigation typology code of PPAs in the City’s AR for CY 2024.
observations.managementAction:
Planned CC adaptation and mitigation projects, programs and activities (PPAs) were implemented in CY 2024. No action was taken relative to the preparation of the M&E Report/AR for LCCAP in CY 2024.

The LCCAP Secretariat did not prepare the M&E report due to difficulty in identifying CC typology code/tag of each PPA in the City’s AR.
observations.reason: The LCCAP was not able to prepare the M&E Report for PPAs relative to LCCAP in CY 2024 due to difficulty in identifying the implemented CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs as reported in the City’s Accomplishment Report (AR) for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management instruct the City Engineer to provide reason/s for the non-compliance of the previous year’s audit recommendations. We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that Management instruct the City Engineer to post project signboards at the locations of the ongoing PPAs. We also reiterated our recommendation that Management submit the following reports: List of all infrastructure projects for implementation for the current year containing the information, at the beginning of the year as required under Item 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; Monitoring report on a monthly basis; and Information to COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboard and/or public notice is already posted on a per project basis.
observations.managementAction: The CEO explained that tarpaulin signboards are installed for all ongoing projects. However, some of these signboards were missing and the prolonged exposure to varying weather conditions caused them to get torn or be easily damaged. Nevertheless, the Office committed to comply with the recommendations.
observations.reason: Some signboards were missing or torn; not all projects had signboards installed during inspection and the required documents were not submitted to the City Auditor’s Office.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that the CAO and the CTO coordinate in order to fully reconcile discrepancies and for the CAO to prepare necessary adjustments to have the correct and reliable RPT and SET Receivable accounts balance as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: The CAO prepared and posted JEV Nos. 2023-03-003024 and 2023-03-000192, both dated March 16, 2023, based on the documents submitted by the CTO as part of the P11,646,094.58 variances noted. Continuous coordination is undertaken.
observations.reason: RPT Receivable and SET Receivable balances have unreconciled balance totaling P202,639,682.05 between the CTO and CAO records as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management commence legal actions or civil remedies such as distraint of personal property or issuance of warrant of levy on real properties relative to the dormant receivables pursuant to Sections 174 to 176, 256 and 270 of RA No. 7160. We also reiterated prior year’s recommendation that CTO conduct regular monitoring of past-due receivables and ensure prompt collections when receivables become due and demandable; CAO conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the receivable accounts.
observations.managementAction: The CTO coordinated with the City Auditor’s Office for write-off of receivables. The CTO monitors the receivables. Regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the receivable accounts are currently being conducted for receivable accounts.
observations.reason: No legal actions or civil remedies were commenced in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: General Services Officer to submit necessary documents to CAO in order to facilitate the recording of all existing PPEs that remained unrecorded in the books of account; Maintain Property Cards for each PPE item; Inventory Committee to verify existence of property items recorded in books but not included in RPCPPE and reconcile with CAO’s records; City Accountant to analyze the PPE items recorded as “Beginning Balance/Reconciling Item” and “2004 Other Asset Balance” and prepare adjusting journal entries.
observations.managementAction: The GSO continuously reconciled with the CAO and submitted the necessary documents to facilitate the recording of existing PPEs. The GSO is maintaining PCs per category of PPE. Continuous coordination with CAO is undertaken to reconcile PPE balances. The CAO prepared adjustments and coordinated with Inventory Committee.
observations.reason: No action was undertaken relative to some recommendations; lump sum amounts presented as reconciling items or beginning balances in the RPCPPE totaling P49,649,419.78; lump sum amounts in PPE Schedule of P169,399,074.26.

observations.recommendation: We commended efforts by CTO but reiterated recommendation that the City adopt strategies such as issuance of warrant of levy and institution of civil action against delinquent real property taxpayers to improve collection effectiveness; intensify existing collection strategies and request assistance from Barangays for tax campaign program.
observations.managementAction: Efforts were undertaken by the CTO to collect receivables such as sending notices to delinquent property owners, posting of list of RPT delinquencies at the City Hall and mass distribution of RPT statement of accounts.
observations.reason: There were still tax delinquencies in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management maximize the services of existing manpower before hiring consultants to avoid additional and unnecessary expenses. We recommended that the Management properly monitor the accomplishments of consultants to ensure adherence to what was required in the contract. We recommended that the Management comply with the requirements of Section 1.0 of DBM Circular Letter No. 2017-09 dated May 16, 2017 and Section 2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 on the hiring of consultants. Reclassify consultants as job order or contractual workers whose job description is not highly technical in nature and use the appropriate account accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Accomplishments were monitored by requiring consultants to prepare ARs. Consultants have been reclassified according to proper account where applicable.
observations.reason: No action was taken relative to some recommendations; for CY 2024, Consultancy Services account had remaining balance indicating Management did not fully utilize existing manpower; no bidding conducted in some hires.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to prepare and submit the required reports pursuant to Sections 7.3.5 and 12.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Resolution No. 11-2020 and post in the website of the City as prescribed under Section 1 of EO No. 662, s. 2007, as amended.
observations.managementAction: APP and PMR were prepared, submitted to GPPB and posted to website.
observations.reason: No APCPI was prepared, submitted to GPPB and posted to website.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City officials to prepare the required report on time and provide the assigned Supervising Auditor and Audit Team Leader with a list of all ongoing PPAs, and those that are to be implemented during the year, for audit validation and verification. Moreover, comply strictly with the provisions of Section 2.2.3 and Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 on the posting of signboards in all infrastructure projects and informing the Audit Team thereof.
observations.managementAction: The CEO committed to comply with the recommendation.
observations.reason: As per inspection of ongoing projects, posting of signboards were partially complied. CEO had not provided the list of all ongoing PPAs, and those that are to be implemented during CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accounting Office ensure that all contributions are remitted in full to the GSIS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG within the prescribed period and make necessary adjustments to correct the balances of Due to PhilHealth and Due to Pag-IBIG. We likewise recommended that the City Personnel Office expedite the reconciliation of their records with the GSIS Service Records to come up with the updated service records so that the full remittance of contributions to the GSIS could be undertaken by the City Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction: The CAO commented that contributions are remitted in full based on billings. The City Personnel Office commented that the reconciliation of records with the GSIS is ongoing, to ensure the updating of service records and the full remittance of employees’ contributions to the respective agency.
observations.reason: The Due to GSIS balance of P17,752,424.15 as of December 31, 2024 includes P3,526,203.96 representing prior years’ contributions and loan amortizations which remain unremitted due to unreconciled records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that the LCCAP be regularly updated, at least annually to reflect changing social, economic, and environmental conditions and emerging issues in compliance with DILG MC No. 2014-135.
observations.managementAction: Verification disclosed that the LCCAP Core Team initiated discussions on updating the LCCAP, however, the necessary activities, such as the CDRA, workshops and writeshops, were not conducted.
observations.reason: The LCCAP of the City of Marikina was not updated in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended for Management to fully comply with RA No. 9729, as amended by RA No. 10174, on the implementation of the City’s projects, programs and activities relative to climate change adaptation and disaster risk reduction and ensure effective monitoring thereof so that the policy of the State in affording full protection and the advancement of the right of the people to a healthful ecology in accord with the rhythm and harmony of nature may be achieved.
observations.managementAction: No action was undertaken by Management relative to the recommendation.
observations.reason: The LCCAP Core Team was not able to prepare the M&E Report for PPAs relative to LCCAP in CY 2024 due to difficulty in identifying the implemented CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs as reported in the City’s AR for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City adopt strategies such as issuance of warrant of levy and institution of civil action against delinquent real property taxpayers to improve collection effectiveness; and to intensify its existing collection strategies. Also, request assistance from the Barangays for the City’s tax campaign program.
observations.managementAction: Efforts were undertaken to intensify tax collection to meet its revenue targets.
observations.reason: Although the City had initiated actions, measures remained inadequate in enforcing collections; resulted in increase of uncollected taxes to P1,004,311,999.41 in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the City Accountant to record the daily collections in their proper income accounts and subsequently recognize them as deposits in the CIB-LCCA to ensure reconciliation between the ledgers and cashbook balances.
We recommended and Management agreed to continue the same in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We reiterated our previous year's recommendations that the LCE instruct the City Treasurer to:
Stop the practice of granting additional CAs to AOs who have not yet settled or properly accounted for their previous CAs; and
Ensure that CAs do not exceed the MCA covered by the AOs’ approved bond, as prescribed by Items 7.1 of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, and 4.8 of the BTR Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated          August 6, 2009.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to reconcile the discrepancies in the records between the recorded balances of RPT and SET Receivable accounts and the total amount of tax delinquencies as indicated in the RPTODL and SRPTDB.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the City Treasurer and propose to the City Council to avail of the tax collection strategies and remedies for tax delinquencies on RPT, such as issuance of warrant of levy and institution of civil actions against delinquent RPT payers pursuant to Section 258 of RA No. 7160 in order to collect outstanding taxes.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the LCE instruct the CHO to coordinate with the accredited HCIs to henceforth maintain monitoring reports indicating the total number of member-patients with complete details on the total billings, amount of claims to PhilHealth, including denied claims, and regularly submit the same on a monthly basis to the City Accountant to facilitate the recording and reconciliation of receivables in the Due from GOCCs account.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the General Services Officer and City Accountant to continue the conduct of regular reconciliation of the inventory account balance and clear any discrepancies immediately to ensure its fair presentation at year-end.
We also reiterated our recommendations that the LCE instruct the General Services Officer to:
Henceforth, properly prepare the RPCI for all types of inventory accounts indicating the complete details on the quantities per card and on hand, and shortage or overage, if any, according to the instructions provided in the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Ensure submission of necessary documents to the CAO such as the Acceptance and Inspection Reports, RIS and SSMI for the CAO to prepare the necessary journal entries.

We recommended that Management direct:
The Inventory Committee to –
Conduct physical count of all PPEs and eliminate the lump sum amounts in the RPCPPE;
Review PPE items with noted discrepancies in coordination with the CAO, including those reported twice, with different acquisition costs compared to CAO records, and that no longer exist, and inform the City Accountant for proper recording in the books;
Reconcile the RPCPPE with CAO records based on the actual physical count of PPE; and
Make the necessary adjustments identified during reconciliation to arrive at the correct PPE balances.
The General Services Officer to forward the Property Cards to City Auditor’s Office so as to facilitate the verification and reconciliation of the balances with the CAO records.
The City Accountant to –
Coordinate with the Inventory Committee to reconcile the PPE balances in the books/PPE Schedule against the reported balances in the RPCPPE as of December 31, 2024 and adjust any discrepancies noted; and
Make the necessary adjustments in the books relative to the overstatement of P104,000.00 in the Office Equipment account for PPE items which were recorded twice.
City provide detailed explanation on why the Balubad Market located at Bagong Sibol, Balubad, Nangka, Marikina City has been inoperative, as well as information on the upcoming bridge project of the DPWH that was affecting its utilization.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Health Officer to review and reconcile the personnel list with the payroll to ensure the accurate inclusion of all eligible claimants.
Likewise, we also recommended that the amounts claimed be thoroughly reviewed and adjusted to comply with the prescribed PhilHealth professional fee rates, in accordance with Sec. 34-A of RA No. 10606 so as to ensure the proper allocation of funds and maintain compliance with the regulations on government PhilHealth reimbursements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Personnel Officer to continue the reconciliation of records with the GSIS so as to come up with the updated service records and full remittance of contributions to the GSIS could be made.
We further recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to determine the composition of the abnormal balance in the SL and effect the necessary adjustment.
We commended the efforts of Management and recommended to continue the full remittance of withheld taxes to the BIR to avoid imposition of penalties, surcharges, or interest.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the City Personnel Officer and City Accountant to reconcile the employees’ records with unremitted amounts so as to facilitate the return/refund of payments to City employees with over-deduction on loan repayments as recorded in the Due to Pag-IBIG account.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to verify the composition of the abnormal balance in the SL and effect the necessary adjustment.

We recommended that Management continue the full remittance of the PhilHealth contributions of City employees within the prescribed period for them to continuously avail the medical care and other benefits of PhilHealth.
We recommended that Management direct the GFPS/GAD Office to continue to optimally utilize the GAD budget.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the City DRRMO to continue to optimally utilize the budget for 70% Mitigation Fund to achieve the purpose of the fund in consonance with Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the CDC to attach the duly approved AIP for 20% DF, including complete details of the PPAs such as the budgeted amount, schedule of implementation and other relevant information and ensure that this is enclosed in the CDC Resolution.

We commended the efforts of the CDC and we recommended and Management agreed to continue the optimal utilization of the 20% DF.
We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the CDC to ensure that all planned and budgeted PPAs are procurement and implementation ready in compliance with Item 3.2.3 of the mentioned JMC.

We commended the efforts of the City for its innovative solid waste management initiatives and recommended the CEMO to continue the same in compliance with the provision of Section 3 of RA No. 9003 and its SWMP.

We reiterated our recommendation to update annually the LCCAP to reflect changing social, economic, and environmental conditions and emerging 
CC issues in compliance with DILG MC No. 2014-135.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the CEMO to:
Prepare the M&E Report/AR for LCCAP for CY 2024 and submit the same to the City Auditor’s Office for verification; and
Coordinate with the City Planning and Development Officer in the inclusion of CC adaptation and mitigation typology code of PPAs in the City’s AR for CY 2024.
We recommended that Management direct the CSWDO to continue to optimally utilize the one percent LCPC Fund.

We recommended that the Management instruct the City Engineer to provide reason/s for the non-compliance of the previous year’s audit recommendations.
We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that Management instruct the City Engineer to post project signboards at the locations of the ongoing PPAs.
We also reiterated our recommendation that Management submit the following reports:
List of all infrastructure projects for implementation for the current year containing the information, at the beginning of the year as required under Item 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004;
Monitoring report on a monthly basis; and
Information to COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboard and/or public notice is already posted on a per project basis.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:
BAC to, henceforth:
Ensure that the APP is properly accomplished by:
Providing specific details for each procurement project;
Identifying a single mode of procurement and avoiding the use of multiple modalities; and
Specifying at least the quarter in which each procurement activity is scheduled to occur.
Align the APP and the AB in accordance with Section 7.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure budget linkage and reconciliation of both documents.
City Budget Officer to, henceforth ensure that the duly approved AB is consistent with the APP by providing the proposed/estimated budget for each PPA pursuant to the provision of Section 7.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the LSB implement a more comprehensive review and planning process for appropriations and expenditures for future budget cycles in accordance with DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017, to ensure that all allocations are nearly close with the actual needs of the students, school and/or teachers.
We also recommended that the City Budget Office and the CAO coordinate and work together to ensure the accurate recording of actual expenditures in the appropriate accounts so as to ensure that expenditures are properly reflected and aligned with the approved budget.

We recommended that the City Treasurer –

Deposit intact daily all collections; and

Refrain from retaining part of collections as a reserve for cash disbursements.
observations.managementAction:
All collections are deposited daily.

Since all collections are deposited daily, no collections were used as cash reserve for disbursements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

CTO to coordinate with the depository banks to support the validity of P3,055,512.94 added to the bank balances by obtaining pertinent documents for submission to the City Accountant for proper adjustment in the books of accounts; and

City Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to record the reconciling items of P3,055,512.94 in the books of accounts as supported with necessary documents forwarded by the City Treasurer from Development Bank of the Philippines and Land Bank of the Philippines.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant made the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts for reconciling items totaling P3,055,512.94, and forwarded copies of Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) Nos. 2024-01-00008, 2024-01-000016, 2024-01-000018 and 2024-02-001625 dated January 4, 17 and February 14, 2024, respectively, its supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to strictly enforce the tax collection strategies and remedies for RPT delinquencies pursuant to Section 258 of RA No. 7160. 

We further recommended that the City Council act accordingly on the List of Real Property Tax Delinquency as of 1st Quarter of CY 2023 which was submitted by CTO on May 9, 2023.
observations.managementAction:
The CTO enforces tax collection strategies by sending notices to delinquent taxpayers.

The City Council enacted ordinance relative to the tax amnesty program.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Accountable Officers to ensure the timely liquidation of cash advances and/or return all unliquidated CAs as of year-end pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: As per validation of Subsidiary Ledgers as of December 31, 2024, all cash advances were liquidated.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that Management direct the:

City Engineer, PDO and other implementing offices to regularly submit the City Accountant all the necessary documents such as the projects’ Certificates of Completion and List of On-going and Completed Infrastructure Projects in order to facilitate the timely recording of completed PPAs to its appropriate PPE accounts.

City Accountant to:

Regularly coordinate with the implementing offices to properly record and monitor all on-going and completed PPAs; and

Make necessary adjustments in the books to reclassify the completed infrastructure projects to appropriate PPE and related depreciation accounts.

The CAO made various adjustments in the CIP accounts.

Remit the full amount of fire safety inspection fees within the period prescribed under Section 11 (b) of Rule IX of the IRR of RA No. 11032, otherwise, provide a copy of the modified Memorandum of Agreement between the City and the BFP which may stipulate the arranged remittance schedule of the collected FSIF; and

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed to continue to utilize the remaining balance of the fire code fees.
observations.managementAction: The Management acknowledged the observation and utilized the remaining balance.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the BAC to prepare, submit and post in the website the PMR of the City pursuant to Section 12.2 of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020.
observations.managementAction: The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) has p continuously prepared and posted the PMR in the website of the City.

observations.managementAction: The Management acknowledged the observation and members of the LCCAP Core Team as per Executive Order (EO) No. 16 approved on December 20, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the CSWDO to continue to optimally utilize the one percent LCPC Fund.
observations.managementAction: The Management acknowledged the observation and committed to comply with the recommendation.

We also recommended that Management upgrade the eGAPS – Real Property Tax Management System and Real Property Assessment System to enable the CTO to generate reports on delinquencies/receivables for specific period, and on adjustments made for submission to the CAO for proper recording in the books.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to strictly enforce the tax collection strategies and remedies for RPT delinquencies pursuant to Section 258 of RA No. 7160.

We further recommended that the City Council act accordingly on the List of Real Property Tax Delinquency as of 1st Quarter of CY 2023 which was submitted by CTO on May 9, 2023.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Office (CHO) to coordinate with the accredited health care institutions to:
Maintain monitoring reports indicating the total number of member patients with billings and amount of claims to PhilHealth; and
Regularly submit all PhilHealth claims for services rendered to member patients with supporting documents to the City Accountant to facilitate the recording of receivables in the Due from GOCCs account.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Accountable Officers to ensure the timely liquidation of cash advances and/or return all unliquidated CAs as of year-end pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:
Commence civil remedies such as distraint of personal property or issuance of warrant of levy on real properties or other applicable actions relative to the dormant receivables pursuant to the provisions of RA 
No. 7160; and
Conduct regular monitoring of past-due receivables and ensure prompt collections when receivables become due and demandable.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to conduct regular analysis and verification of the Other Receivables account so as to identify the details and collectability of all receivable items which remained dormant in the books.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
General Services Officer to –
Properly prepare the RPCI for all types of inventory accounts indicating the complete details on the quantities per card and on hand, and shortage or overage, if any, according to the instructions provided in the MNGAS for LGUs;
Prepare the RPCI using the moving average method pursuant to Section 115 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Ensure submission of necessary documents to the CAO such as the RIS and SSMI in order for the CAO to prepare necessary journal entries.
City Accountant and General Services Officer to –
Analyze and identify the discrepancies between the Inventory Schedule and RPCI and take necessary actions to resolve the same; and
Conduct regular reconciliation of the inventory balance account and clear any discrepancies immediately, if any, to ensure fair presentation of the inventory accounts at year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
The Inventory Committee to –
Conduct the required physical count of all PPE and ensure the correct recording of the same in the RPCPPE and eliminate the lump sum amounts in the RPCPPE;
Reconcile the RPCPPE with CAO records based on the actual physical count of PPE; and
Make the necessary adjustments identified during reconciliation to arrive at the correct PPE balances.
The General Services Officer to maintain PCs for all PPE items to accurately track the PPE and reconcile the same with the CAO records;
The City Accountant to –
Coordinate with the Inventory Committee to reconcile the PPE balances in the books/PPE Schedule against the reported balances in the RPCPPE as of December 31, 2023;
Coordinate with the Land Management Office and adjust the books accordingly relative to the distribution of land items to beneficiaries and their payments with complete documentation; and
Make the necessary adjustments in the books pertaining to the unused equipment with Accumulated Depreciation totaling P1,544,519.72.
City Health Office to ensure that procurement of equipment is meticulously planned to guarantee its immediate usage.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management direct the:
City Engineer, PDO and other implementing offices to regularly submit the City Accountant all the necessary documents such as the projects’ Certificates of Completion and List of On-going and Completed Infrastructure Projects in order to facilitate the timely recording of completed PPAs to its appropriate PPE accounts.
City Accountant to:
Regularly coordinate with the implementing offices to properly record and monitor all on-going and completed PPAs; and
Make necessary adjustments in the books to reclassify the completed infrastructure projects to appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) and related depreciation accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:
Remit the full amount of fire safety inspection fees within the period prescribed under Section 11 (b) of Rule IX of the IRR of RA No. 11032, otherwise, provide a copy of the modified Memorandum of Agreement between the City and the BFP which may stipulate the arranged remittance schedule of the collected FSIF; and
Coordinate with the City Accountant to trace the unremitted balance of P485,569.99 in the records to identify the months to which it pertains and immediately remit the same to the BFP.
We recommended and the Management agreed to continue to utilize the remaining balance of the fire code fees.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the BAC to prepare, submit and post in the website the PMR of the City pursuant to Section 12.2 of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to post project signboards on the place where the PPAs are located and to submit:
The list of all ongoing infrastructure projects and those for implementation for the current year containing the information required under Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004;
The monitoring report on a monthly basis; and
Information to COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboard and/or public notice is already posted on a per project basis.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the recommendations that the:
LCCAP Core Team initiate discussions on updating the LCCAP of the City pursuant to DILG MC No. 2014-135; and
LCCAP be regularly and completely updated, at least annually to reflect changing social, economic, and environmental conditions and emerging issues in compliance with DILG MC No. 2014-135.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
Implementing offices to ensure that the planned climate change adaptation and mitigation PPAs as indicated in the plan and budget of the City are implemented in compliance with Section 2 of RA No. 9729, as amended by RA No. 10174; and
LCCAP Secretariat, in close coordination with the implementing offices, to prepare the M&E Plan/Report to include all the CC adaptation and mitigation PPAs for implementation of the City for the year and specify the complete details such as the allocated budget, actual cost or expenditure and status of PPAs to facilitate monitoring of utilization and implementation of PPAs as prescribed by DILG MC No. 2014-135 dated October 21, 2014.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDC to:
Adequately plan the specific PPAs funded under the 20% DF and ensure that these are explicitly stated/enclosed in the CDC Resolution;
Ensure that all planned and budgeted PPAs are procurement-and-implementation-ready so that potential factors for delays can be identified and circumvented;
Ensure the proper preparation of the AR for 20% DF to accurately reflect the implementation of priority development PPAs; and
Maintain constant coordination with the implementing offices and other government offices specifically DPWH to facilitate the timely and full implementation of PPAs pursuant to the provisions of Section 109 of 
RA No. 7160 and DBM-DOF- DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.
We further recommended that Management direct the CPDO to align AIP and the Annual Budget to avoid confusion in the implementation of priority development PPAs.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management instruct the City’s SWMB to update its 10-year SWMP annually and ensure that the pertinent components are included in the SWMP pursuant to Sections 16 and 17 of 
RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CEMO in the submission of the City’s updated COVID-19 waste management plan upon its finalization and approval to facilitate its review and ascertain compliance with the proper handling and management of all COVID-19 related health care waste.
We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City’s SWMB to comply with the requirements under Section 23 of RA No. 9003 that all collectors and other personnel directly dealing with collection of solid waste shall be equipped with personal protective equipment to protect them from the hazards of handling solid waste.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the CSWDO to properly prepare the LCPC AR to reflect the correct amount of funding and expenditures and to include end-results of the implemented PPAs pursuant to DILG MC Nos. 2008-126 and 2021-039.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CSWDO to properly plan the procurement activities pursuant to provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, in order to avoid reimbursement of expenditures.

We commended the Management for utilizing 75 percent of its GAD budget in accordance with its approved GPB, and we further recommended that Management continue to implement PPAs that will address the City’s identified gender issues.
We recommended that Management to submit the GPB and GAD AR within the prescribed period in COA Circular No. 2014-001 so that the timely review and evaluation may be undertaken.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the City DRRM Office to continue to optimally utilize the budget for 70% Mitigation Fund to achieve the purpose for which the fund is created pursuant to Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We reiterated our recommendation and the Management agreed to direct the City DRRMO to provide an LDRRMFIP indicating the details of programs, projects and activities to be charged in the unexpended LDRRMF balance recorded in the TL–DRRMF account in the TF Books pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
We recommended that Local School Board utilize the SEF based on the purposes for which the fund was created.

We recommended that the Schools Division Superintendent submit request for procurement ahead of time to enable the GSO and/or the BAC to appropriately act on such requests and in accordance with of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
City Personnel Officer expedite the reconciliation of records with the GSIS to come up with the updated service records so that the full remittance of contributions to the GSIS may be made; and
City Accountant to ensure that all contributions are remitted in full to the GSIS and Pag-IBIG within the prescribed period, and to facilitate the return/refund of payments to City employees with over-deduction on loan repayments as recorded in the Due to Pag-IBIG and identify the causes of non-remittance for the remaining balance of unremitted contributions.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Accountant continue to remit the full amount of withheld PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period in order that the City employees may continuously avail the medical care and other benefits with PhilHealth.

We recommended that the City Mayor to direct the City Treasure to ensure that the remittance to the BIR should be the full amount of the withheld taxes and that it should be made within the period prescribed to avoid surcharge and interest.
We recommended that Management require the Accounting and Treasury Offices to prioritize the regular reconciliation of their records before closing the books to eliminate discrepancies.

observations.recommendation:
8. We recommended that the Management direct the:

        a. City Treasurer’s Office –


           • Coordinate with the Development Bank  of  the  Philippines  to
             ascertain the validity of P669,284.78 by  obtaining  pertinent
             documents that supports its existence;


           •  Submit  all  obtained  documents  to  the  CAO   for   proper
             adjustment/s in the books of accounts;

        b. City Accounting Office to –


           • Prepare journal entry vouchers to record the reconciling items
             in the books of accounts as supported with  proper  documents;
             and


           • Record the P669,284.78 in the books of  accounts  and  prepare
             necessary  adjustments,  if  needed,  upon  receipt   of   the
             supporting documents from the City Treasurer’s Office.

1. We recommended Management to:

        a. Ensure that all transactions threshed out from  the  Petty  Cash
           Funds are not regular expenses of  the  City  like  the  various
           assistance granted to its constituents; and


        b. Henceforth, adherence to the provisions prescribed under Section
           89 of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 and COA Circular
           No. 2012-01 be strictly observed.

16. We recommended that:

        a. The concerned accountable officers  immediately  liquidate  their
           cash advances in accordance with Item Nos. 4.1, 5.1, 5.7 and  5.8
           of COA Circular No. 97-002;


        b. The City Accountant identify  the  payees  of  dishonored  checks
           totalling  P503,970.97  recorded  under  the  Other   Receivables
           account in order to comply with the audit recommendations on  the
           audit of dormant receivable accounts;


        c. The City Accountant to attach paid payrolls for  the  liquidation
           of AO No. 1 covering salaries of teachers  amounting  P59,250.00;
           and


        d. We recommended  that  the  City  Accountant  draw  Journal  Entry
           Voucher to re-classify account balances, following the  suggested
           journal entries presented below, and henceforth, use  appropriate
           accounts prescribed in the Chart of Accounts under GAM for  LGUs,
           Volume III, in recording cash advances.

|Advances to Special Disbursing     |69,750.00  |        |
|Officers                           |           |        |
|Advances for Operating Expenses    |212,575.00 |        |
|       Other Receivables           |           |282,325.|
|                                   |           |00      |
|To re-classify account balances to |           |        |
|conform with the Chart of  Accounts|           |        |
|under GAM, Volume III.             |           |        |

9. We reiterated our  prior  year’s  recommendation  that  the  CAO  to
        coordinate with the  CTO  in  order  to  reconcile  the  discrepancy
        between the recorded balances of RPT  and  SET  Receivable  accounts
        against the total amount  of  tax  delinquencies  indicated  in  the
        RPTODL. Also, for the CAO to prepare the  necessary  adjustments  to
        have the correct  and  reliable  RPT  and  SET  Receivable  accounts
        balance as of year-end.

9.   We recommended that:

        a. the CTO –


           • Assess the collectibility of  dormant  receivables  and  exert
             efforts to warrant collection especially  those  debtors  with
             substantial unpaid obligations;


           • Conduct regular monitoring of past-due receivables and  ensure
             prompt  collection  as  the   receivables   become   due   and
             demandable;

        b. the CAO –


           • Conduct  regular  and  periodic  verification,  analysis,  and
             validation of the receivable accounts;


           • Send demand letter to the debtors or other  possible remedies
             to collect the dormant receivables;


           • Prepare aging of dormant receivables for  better  tracking  of
             uncollected receivables; and

        c. the CTO and the CAO coordinate with each other  to  evaluate  if
           any dormant receivable is impaired and if a request  for  write-
           off is necessary pursuant  to  the  guidelines  and  documentary
           requirements under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

18. We recommended that the City Mayor require:

          a. The CAO to –


            • Record all inventory purchases  as  asset  in  the  books  of
              accounts following the Perpetual Inventory Method as required
              under Section 51 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and


            • Prepare necessary JEVs to record all inventory items existing
              in the RPCI but not recorded in the books, including the cost
              of the remaining 40 donated wheelchairs.


          b. The GSO to –


            • Ensure submission of necessary reports/documents to  the  CAO
              within the deadline set, in order for the CAO to be  able  to
              prepare necessary journal entries on time;


            • Provide the CAO details of all inventory items to  facilitate
              the recording in the books of accounts; and


            • Prepare separate RPCI for General Fund and Special  Education
              Fund (SEF) for easy reconciliation.


          c. CAO and GSO to –


            • Analyze and  identify  discrepancies  of  inventory  balances
              between the two records and take necessary action to  resolve
              the discrepancies; and

        d. Henceforth, conduct  regular  reconciliation  of  the  inventory
           balance account to ensure fair  presentation  of  the  inventory
           accounts.

1. We recommended that the City Accountant ensure adherence to  Section
        114 of the MNGAS for LGUs on the proper account  treatment  for  the
        purchase of supplies  and  materials,  particularly,  Textbooks  and
        Instructional Materials and that all  recordings  to  Textbooks  and
        Instructional Materials Expense account are  sufficiently  supported
        with the necessary RIS and SSMI.

     2. We also recommended that the General Services  Officer  prepare  and
        submit to the City Accountant the monthly RSMI together with the RIS
        as bases by the  latter  to  record  in  the  accounting  books  the
        issuances made, if any, of instructional materials purchased  in  CY
        2021 totaling to P121,542,715.40, in pursuant to Section 121 of  the
        MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

5. We recommended that the Officer-in-Charge,  Office  of  the  Schools
        Division Superintendent, provide  in  the  department’s  procurement
        plan, the exact and/or reasonable number of  textbooks/instructional
        materials, construction materials, and other supplies and  materials
        needed in the operations of  schools  for  one  fiscal  year  to  be
        consumed/issued/distributed within a determinable period of time  to
        avoid overstocking, so as to comply with Section 19 of COA  Circular
        No. 92-386, and avoid excessive expenditures of government funds.

6. We recommended that the GSO  immediately  submit  the  corresponding
        SSMI to the Accounting Office for recording and  come  up  with  the
        correct balances of the affected accounts as of year-end.

9. We recommended that the:

        a. City Health Office to submit promptly all necessary documents  to
           CAO in order to facilitate the timely recording of  the  receipts
           and utilization of donations in-kind;


        b. CAO  to  record  all  receipts  of  donations  in  kind  and  its
           subsequent utilization in the books of accounts; and


        c. Henceforth, comply with the provisions of Item 4A of Section  36,
           Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I  and  Paragraph  27  of
           IPSAS 1 regarding the proper recording of receipt and utilization
           of donations in kind.

20. We also recommended that the:

        a. General Services Officer to –


           • Submit promptly to CAO of all necessary documents in order  to
             facilitate the timely recording of the newly acquired PPEs,
             including those recorded in the RPCPPE but still not  recorded
             in the books of accounts;


           • Complete the valuation of all PPE items without  corresponding
             amounts;


        b. Inventory Committee to verify/investigate the  existence  of  the
           property items that were recorded in the books of accounts, in
           order to facilitate inclusion of all items still not included in
           the RPCPPE;


        c. City Accountant to –


           • Prepare  journal  entry  vouchers  to  record  the  PPE  items
             included in the RPCPPE but still unrecorded in  the  books  of
             accounts upon receipt of supporting documents from GSO;

           • Analyze the reconciling item balances in the PPE accounts and,
             accordingly, prepare adjusting journal entries to reflect  the
             correct balances of the affected accounts; and

           • Exert efforts to locate the documents showing details  of  the
             SL balances that are reported in lump;


        d. City Accountant and General Services Officer to –


           • Reconcile and verify  the  correct  acquisition  cost  of  the
             affected PPE items and prepare the necessary  adjustments,  if
             necessary;


           • Have better coordination in reconciling their records in order
             to eliminate the variances in all the affected  PPE  accounts;
             and


           • To evaluate if the one-time cleansing of  PPE  balances  under
             COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, is warranted
             to reconcile all the discrepancies in the PPE accounts.

10. We recommended that the City Mayor require:

        a. The City Engineering Office to expedite the processing of  claims
           of the completed projects  by  contract  totaling  P97,753,420.36
           (P49,664,539.48 and P48,088,880.88)  and  issue  certificates  of
           completion for projects implemented by administration involving a
           total amount of P60,897,056.71 (P58,310,337.12,  P781,236.50  and
           P1,805,483.09);


        b. The CAO to coordinate with the City Engineering  Office  for  the
           documents necessary to reclassify the aforementioned projects  to
           their  proper  accounts  and  record  the  related   Depreciation
           Expense, Accumulated Depreciation and Accumulated Surplus;


        c. We likewise  recommend  the  CAO  to  coordinate  with  the  City
           Engineering Office to verify  the  understatement  in  the  total
           amount of P723,090.22 on six completed road projects and  prepare
           the adjusting entries to close it to the proper asset account;


        d. Record the completed projects identified as dormant for two years
           with  a  total  amount  of  P30,607,239.54  (P30,609,939.54  less
           P2,700.00 that should be recorded as repairs and maintenance)  to
           the  appropriate  PPE  account  after  securing   the   pertinent
           documents from the City Engineering Office; and


        e. Adjust the amount of P2,700.00 to Accumulated Surplus  to  record
           the cost of repairs and maintenance incurred in CY 2019.

1. We recommended that the City Accountant to:


        a.  Verify  and  send  notices  to  the  creditors  of  said  unpaid
           obligations to establish the validity of the  claims,  otherwise,
           revert back the amount of P14,920,203.34  to  the  unappropriated
           surplus of the General Fund to be used for additional  source  of
           supplemental budget; and


        b. Prepare the  necessary  adjusting  entries  to  revert  back  the
           P6,444,174.53 excessive set-up of Accounts Payable.

9. We recommended that Management:


        a. Strictly adhere to the provisions of Paragraph 5.2 of DBM NBC
           No. 2007-510 on the grant of honoraria to officials  and  members
           of the Board of Trustees of PLMAR;

        b. Require the concerned officials and members of the BOT to  refund
           the overpayment received by them;

        c. Require the refund  from  the  recipients  of  the  corresponding
           amount of tax that should have been withheld be refunded  by  the
           recipients and henceforth, the appropriate taxes be withheld from
           the payment of honoraria  to  the  BOT  in  accordance  with  the
           Revised Withholding Tax Table of the BIR; and


        d. Require the Secretary of the BOT of PLMAR to provide  the  Office
           of the City Auditor with copies of the documents enumerated under
           Paragraph 5.7.3 of  COA  Circular  No.  2012-001  to  ensure  the
           validity and  legality  of  the  transaction  and  to  avoid  the
           issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.

9. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Personnel  Office
        to stop granting TA to City Officials and Department Heads  who  are
        assigned with government  service  vehicles  or  make  corresponding
        proportional deductions to the TA of all who  are  using  government
        vehicles pursuant to Local Budget Circular No. 103 and COA Circular
        No. 2000-005, otherwise this Office will  be  constrained  to  issue
        Notice of Disallowance.

    10. We further recommended to stop the grant of TA to all City Officials
        and Department Heads whose unit or subordinate under  him  has  been
        assigned government service vehicle.

14. We recommended that Management to:

        a. Strictly adhere to the provisions prescribed under Item No. 6,
           Section 4 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001  requiring
           that  all  transactions  should  be   supported   with   complete
           documentations;

        b. Limit spending for representation expenses within the purview  of
           official meetings/conferences/entertainments  as  provided  under
           COA Circular No. 2004-008;

        c. Strictly require complete documentation to  support  disbursement
           charged  against  representation  expenses  such  as  notice   of
           meeting/conferences/entertainment    activities,    agenda    for
           meetings, activity  or  program  design,  list  of  participants,
           distribution list or attendance sheets, minutes of  meetings  and
           brief post-activity report should be attached in every payment;

        d. Include planned procurement with provision for catering services,
           meals and snacks during meetings/conferences and so the same  can
           be consolidated to the Annual Procurement Plan as required  under
           RA No. 9184;

        e. Henceforth, the  cost  of  meals  and  snacks  to  be  spent  for
           entertaining visitor/guests  for  the  corresponding  offices  be
           charged against  the  monthly  representation  allowance  of  the
           department heads which is the very purpose of the grant  of  said
           allowance;

        f. Explain the duplication of photos attached for April 29  and  30,
           2021 and for July 26, August 1 to 15 and September 1 to 15, 2021;
           and

        g. Require the agency official responsible for accepting  deliveries
           of procured items to, within twenty-four  (24)  hours  from  such
           acceptance, notify the Auditor  of  the  time  and  date  of  the
           scheduled deliveries as  prescribed  under  Section  A.2  of  COA
           Circular
           No. 1996-010 dated August 15, 1996.

    15. We likewise recommended that the City Accounting  Office  reclassify
        the amount  of  P275,421.21  from  Representation  Expense  to  Food
        Expense Account.

23. We recommended that the City Mayor to:

        a. Instruct the Office of the City Administration who took charge in
           the distribution of the financial assistance under the Office  of
           the City Mayor to submit proof on the need for  assistance,  such
           as but not limited to, the following:


           • Certificate of indigency or in need  of  assistance  from  the
             barangay;
           • Medical certificate for medical assistance;
           • Death certificate for burial assistance;
           • Certificate of separation from or suspension of work; and
           • Distribution list for the food supplies distributed to  COVID-
             19 patients and their families.

        b. Ensure that all requests for assistance should pass  through  the
           CSWDO for verification or screening;

        c. Instruct the CSWDO to coordinate with  the  Office  of  the  City
           Mayor on matters pertaining to request for financial assistance;

        d. Instruct the office/personnel in charge of  the  distribution  of
           the food supplies to COVID-19  patients  and  their  families  to
           submit the distribution list to the Audit Team for verification;

        e. Instruct the OSCA to update their data base and  investigate  why
           the remaining 37% Senior Citizen did not avail the Cash Gift  for
           the proper implementation of SP Ordinance No. 60 on the grant  of
           birthday cash gift to all senior citizens of the City;

        f. Instruct the City Accounting Office to reclassify the  amount  of
           P37,933,082.98 from Donations to Food Supplies  Expense  Account;
           and

        g. Henceforth, for Management to strictly adhere to the implementing
           guidelines of  the  City  on  the  grant  of  assistance  to  its
           constituents under EO No. 02 of the Office of the City Mayor,  in
           relation to EO
           No. 221, s. 2003, and DSWD Administrative Order No. 5,
           s.  2008,  particularly  the  required  verification  or   strict
           screening by the CSWDO of the request  for  assistance,  ensuring
           that relevant supporting documents are attached, and adhering  to
           the  rules  in  determining  the  amounts  to   be   granted   to
           beneficiaries, to prevent possible abuse or misuse of the funds.

12. We recommended that City Mayor to instruct the:

        a.  City  Treasurer,  in  coordination  with  the  concerned  Punong
           Barangay, to:


           • require the identified beneficiaries not qualified for SAP-ESP
             to refund the amount given to them; and


           • remit/return to DSWD the amount collected  from  the  refunded
             SAP-ESP from the unqualified beneficiaries;


        b.  City Social Welfare Office in coordination  with  the  concerned
           Punong Barangay to provide supporting documents as proof that the
           paid beneficiaries without valid IDs, without information of work
           and income in the SAC, and not included in the  Master  List  are
           eligible for the cash subsidy.

7. We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that  the  City  adopt
        strategies such as issuance of warrant of levy  and  institution  of
        civil action against delinquent real property taxpayers  to  improve
        collection effectiveness; and to intensify its  existing  collection
        strategies. Also, request assistance  from  the  Barangays  for  the
        City’s tax campaign program, particularly in  the  dissemination  of
        Notices of Delinquencies and demand letters, and serve  warrants  of
        levy to the delinquent real property owners within their territorial
        jurisdiction.

7. We recommended that the City Mayor  instruct  the  City  Treasurer’s
        Office to ensure timely remittance of barangays’ share from the  RPT
        collections within five (5) days, after the end of each quarter,  in
        compliance to Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160 and Section 15  of  the
        MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

4. We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the Bids
        and Awards Committee (BAC)/BAC Secretariat to properly  prepare  the
        Purchase Orders by  completing  all  the  necessary  information  as
        required under COA Circular No. 96-010.

4. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation  that  the  General
        Services Offices submit Notices of Deliveries within 24  hours  from
        acceptance of deliveries in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010.

6. We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

        a. Procurement Unit, General Services Office to  monitor  compliance
           by the supplier of  the  delivery  terms  of  procured  items  as
           specified in the Purchase Orders to ensure prompt  and  effective
           delivery of public services in  compliance  with  the  Manual  of
           Procedures for the Procurement of Goods and Services, Volume  II;
           and

        b. Procurement Unit, General Services Office and the City Accounting
           Office to impose liquidated damages on late deliveries and deduct
           from any amount due the suppliers as provided under Items 3.1 and
           3.2 of Annex D, RIRR of RA No. 9184,  to  ensure  that  suppliers
           strictly comply with the terms of the contract.

11. We recommended that the City Mayor:


        a. Instruct the BAC  to  submit  the  following  lacking  supporting
           documents:


           • Mayor’s Permit;
           • Business/Income Tax Returns;
           • Omnibus Sworn Statement;
           • Certification or equivalent document showing that they exerted
             efforts to secure the most  advantageous  price  to  the  City
             Government based on existing  price  data  of  the  City,  the
             Department of Trade and Industry or other relevant agencies or
             preliminary  market  scanning  done  by  the  agency   showing
             prevailing market prices and practice; and

        b. Henceforth, require the BAC  to  ensure  the  submission  of  the
           required documentation before awarding or before payment  as  the
           case maybe, in compliance with GPPB Circular  No.  01-2020,  GPPB
           Resolution
           No. 18-2020, Consolidated Guidelines for the Alternative  Methods
           of Procurement (Annex “H”) of the RIRR of RA  No.  9184  and  COA
           Circular No. 2012-001.

13. We recommended that:

        a. the City Accounting Office  ensure  that  all  contributions  are
           remitted in full to the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG within  the
           prescribed period; and


        b. the City Personnel Office expedite the  reconciliation  of  their
           records with the GSIS Service Records to come up with the updated
           service records so that the full remittance of  contributions  to
           the GSIS could be undertaken by the City Accounting Office.

7. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CSWMB  to  review  and
        update the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of  the  City  every
        two years or as the need arises in compliance with  Section  4  (g),
        Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003,  to ensure the sustainability,
        viability and effectiveness of the solid  waste  management  of  the
        City.

30. We reiterated prior year’s recommendation that the City Mayor ensure
        that the SEF is utilized only for purposes provided  under  Sections
        100(c) and 272 of Local Government Code of 1991 or RA No. 7160,  and
        consider  only   programs/projects   enumerated   thereat   in   the
        preparation of the SEF Budget.

    31. We also recommended that Management stop the  practice  of  charging
        expenses contrary to purpose for which  the  SEF  was  created,  and
        require the Disbursing Officers with unliquidated cash  advances  to
        liquidate their cash advances immediately.

21. We recommended that the:

        a. GAD Focal Point System  use  the  GAD  Plan  and  Budget  in  the
           formulation of the  GAD  AR  and  follow  the  guide  in  in  the
           preparation of the GAD AR as provided under  Section  4.C.8  PCW-
           DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as  amended,  and  to  attach  the
           documents required  under  the  JMC,  to  facilitate  the  proper
           evaluation and assessment of the implementation of the GAD PPAs;

        b. Management reflect the GAD programs, projects and  activities  as
           indicated in the LGU  GPB  in  the  AIP  and  undertake  adequate
           planning and budgeting to ensure effective  gender  mainstreaming
           in the local development plans in accordance with  PCW-DILG-NEDA-
           JMC No.2013-01;

        c. GFPS to regularly monitor if the GAD budget is  being  spent  for
           what it was  intended  for  and  check  on  the  actual  cost  of
           implementing the GAD activities, and to ensure the effective  and
           efficient implementation of the GAD PPAs and that the GAD  budget
           is being judiciously utilized pursuant to Item 4 of PCW-DILG-NEDA-
           JMC
           No. 2013-01; and


        d. GAD Database containing gender statistics  and  sex-disaggregated
           be established  to  serve  as  the  basis  for  gender-responsive
           planning, programming and policy formulation,  and  we  reiterate
           our  previous  recommendation  that  the   City   undertake   the
           procurement  of  the  software  as  this  is  necessary  in   the
           updating/maintenance  of  the  GAD  database  as  required  under
           Section 37 (D), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No.  9710  and  JMC  No.
           2013-01.

11. We recommended that  Management  ensure  compliance  with  Item  III
        (A.3.2) of MC No. 2008-126  by  formulating  the  required  AWFP  to
        assure  that  the  City’s  LCPC  is   adequately   guided   with   a
        comprehensive plan on juvenile delinquency prevention, and to enable
        the City to properly assess the functionality of the CLCPC.

8. We recommended that Management ensure compliance with  COA  Circular
        No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 by  formulating  the
        required LDDDMFIP and annual report on the utilization of the LDRRMF
        to ensure that the City is guided in the allocation and use  of  the
        LDRRMF, and that accountability and transparency on the use  thereof
        may be enhanced.

7. We recommended for Management to ensure that  PPAs  incorporated  in
        the   AIP   be   provided   or   allocated   with   funds   in   the
        annual/supplementary budget to warrant the implementation thereof as
        in this case to contain the spread and eliminate the threat of COVID-
        19, pursuant to DBM Local Budget Circular No.  124  and  NDRRMC-DBM-
        DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1.

8. We reiterated our  previous  recommendations  that  the  City  Mayor
        require the:

        a. City Accountant to record the cash donations from  local  sources
           as Trust Liability-DRRMF under the Trust Fund book and prepare  a
           separate Subsidiary Ledger for cash donations received  amounting
           to P100,000.00 and above per donor per purpose  and  those  below
           P100,000.00 be  grouped  and  posted  in  one  Subsidiary  Ledger
           labeled as “Various Donors” per purpose in  compliance  with  COA
           Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2012-002;

        b. City Budget Officer and the City  Accountant  to  submit  to  the
           Audit Group the Report on the Receipt  and  Utilization  of  Cash
           Donations as required under Item V.A.1 of COA Circular No.  2014-
           002; and

        c. City Budget Officer  and  the  City  Accountant  to  publish  the
           required reports in the website of the City  in  compliance  with
           Item V.C of COA Circular No. 2014-002.

17. We recommended that Management adequately plan and monitor the  PPAs
        funded under the 20% DF, and ensure that PPAs under the
        20% DF are timely and fully implemented.

    18. We also recommended that the:

        a. City Planning and Development Office to:


           • program PPAs based on the amount of budget for the 20% DF;


           • ensure that all the PPAs under the 20% DF are included in  the
             AIP and its subsequent implementation in the AAR; and


           • prepare consolidated quarterly reports to properly monitor and
             assess the status and utilization of each PPA, consistent with
             the reporting requirements prescribed  by  the  DOF-Bureau  of
             Local Government Finance.

        b. City Engineering Office and City Parks and Development Office  to
           ensure timely implementation of PPAs; and

        c. Management to, henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of
           DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, and DBM-DOF-DILG
           JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, to optimally contribute to  the
           attainment   of   desirable   socio-economic   development    and
           environmental management targets and outcomes of the City.

1.  We  recommended  that  the  Management  immediately   enforce   the
        settlement of all audit disallowances in  compliance  with  Sections
