Specify the agenda of each meeting; and
Support payments for food and drinks with minutes of meetings specifying the date, time and agenda of the meetings and pictures or documentation of the activities.
We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to see to it that payments are made only on completely and properly documented claims in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.
Management comment:
The Audit Team noted the positive response by management on the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Accountant take up the necessary adjustments.  Henceforth, compliance with the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 is enjoined for fair presentation of the affected accounts.
Management comment:
The City Accountant explained that disbursements were recorded in the books of accounts based on the accounts used by the City Budget Office when allocating the budget for the proposed expenses. She further explained that much as they wanted to comply with the audit recommendations, they can no longer draw journal entries as the books of accounts for CY 2019 was already closed.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Team has duly noted Management comment on the matter. It encouraged, however, that both the City Accounting and Budget Offices record government transactions in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts.

Management comment:
Management assured taking actions on the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to abide with the posting requirement of Request for Price Quotation in the PhilGEPS website, website of the City and any conspicuous place within the premises of the City reserved for this purpose in compliance with Section 54.2 and Item V.8 of Annex H of the IRR of RA No. 9184, to enable wider coverage of participation of bonafide suppliers who could offer lower prices, which are more advantageous to the government.
Management comment:
The BAC committed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Require the BAC to submit the required documentation for the SVP in compliance with the Consolidated Guidelines for the Alternative Methods of Procurement Annex (“H”) of the IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Advise the BAC to stop the practice of recommending the award of contracts to suppliers who fail to comply with the required documentation.
Management comment:
The BAC assured compliance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee to adopt public bidding on purchases involving an ABC of more than P1.00 million, except when circumstances warrant other modes of procurement, in compliance with Sections 10 and 53.9 and Annex “H” of the IRR of RA No. 9184.
Management comment:
The BAC has duly noted the audit observations and committed compliance with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Officer to properly prepare the Purchase Orders by indicating the necessary information in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010 and require the supplier to indicate the date of signing on the Conforme portion of the PO.
Management comment:
The City General Services Officer informed that a memorandum was already issued requiring the person/s responsible to completely and properly accomplish the PO/contracts.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the GSO submit Notices of Deliveries within 24 hours from acceptance of deliveries in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010.
Management comment:
The GSO informed that a memorandum was issued requiring the person responsible to submit the Notice of Delivery to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local School Board of Marikina City to ensure that the SEF is utilized only for purposes provided under Sections 100 (c) and 272 of RA No. 7160 and consider only the programs/projects enumerated thereat in the preparation of the SEF budget.
We also recommended that the amount utilized for ineligible SEF expenses be replenished from the GF.
Management comment:
The Management assured that they will take appropriate actions on the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Development Council, headed by the City Mayor, and the Local Finance Committee:
Ensure that charges against the 20% DF is in accordance with Item        No. 3.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01; and
Facilitate the refund of the amount totaling P51,815,693.89 to the 20%DF from the appropriate fund of the City.
Management comment:
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Require the City Budget Officer to see to it that payment of the interests, which are not considered as development projects, should be charged to the appropriate fund; and
Advise the City Development Council to ensure that the 20% DF is utilized only for purposes provided under Item No. 3 of the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-01.
Management comment:
Management assured compliance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the Head of the GFPS to properly plan and identify GAD PPAs by determining specific outcomes and setting PPAs timelines, pursuant to Section 43 of RA No. 9710 and consider the procurement of the software for the updating/maintenance of the GAD database for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation as required under Section 37 (D) Rule VI of the IRR of 
RA No. 9710.
Management comment:
The Head, GFPS acknowledged the recommendation of the Team on GAD Fund utilization and committed to focus more on programs/ projects/activities in the GAD Plan and Budget that have specific outcomes or are implementable.  She further explained that in view of the cost involved in procuring the software for sex disaggregated database and since the database could be updated manually by obtaining the desired information from the Philippine Statistics Office, the plan to procure the software was set aside first in CY 2019.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the GAD Focal Point System Technical Working Group ensure the timely submission of the GPB so that the DILG could release the reviewed GPB in accordance with the schedule prescribed under Item 4 of the same JMC and the 
City Planning and Development Office to include the endorsed GPB PPAs in the AIP before the budget preparation.
Management comment:
The Head, GFPS Secretariat, committed to improve the timeliness on the submission of GPB considering that in CY 2020 implementation of DILG GPB Monitoring system will require online filing.

We recommended that the City Mayor issue an Executive Order establishing a GAD M&E System as required under Item Nos. 3.4, 5.1 and 5.2 of 
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
Management comment:
The Head, GFPS Secretariat informed that an Executive Order to establish a GAD M&E System will be drafted for review and consideration of the City Mayor.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to facilitate the submission of the required documents to the Audit Team and henceforth, ensure that only Disbursement Vouchers with complete documentation are approved and paid, in compliance with Section 4 (6) of PD No.  1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
Management comment:
The City Accountant assured complete documentation for the disbursement amounting to P11,579,843.92.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Budget Officer to:
Refrain from charging against the LDRRMF projects and activities that are not included or related to the DRRM activities as provided in 
Section 21 of RA 10121, Item Nos. 4.0 and 5.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and Item 3.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
Return to the LDRRMF the total amount of P48,720,660.00 utilized for non-DRRM related activities.  Such amount should be charged against the appropriate funds of the City.
Management comment:
The City Accountant and the City Budget Officer assured compliance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CSWMB to review and update the City’s Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan every two years or as the need arises in compliance with Section 4 (g) Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
Management comment:
The Team has noted the positive response of the CEMO on the audit recommendation. He informed, that the required activities will be undertaken in the ensuing year to update the Ten-Year SWMP.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City adopt strategies such as issuance of warrant of levy and institution of civil action against delinquent real property taxpayers to improve collection efficiency. Also, request assistance of the Barangays for the City’s tax campaign program, particularly in the dissemination of Notices of Delinquencies and serve warrants of levy to the delinquent real property owners within their territorial jurisdiction.
Management comment:
The City Treasurer has duly noted the audit observation and assured taking action to implement the audit recommendations.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City through the Office of the Mayor facilitate the creation of the IASO as per City Ordinance No. 018 series of 2016, and adopt the Internal Audit Manual for LGUs as guide in the operation of the IAS.
Management comment:
The City Budget Officer informed that during CY 2019 the budget for IASO has already been provided. Organizational set-up for the IASO is targeted to be completed in CY 2020.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the CAO to see to it that all taxes withheld are remitted in full to the BIR within the prescribed period in compliance with Sections 2.58, 2.81 and 5.116 of Revenue Regulation 
No. 2-98.
Management comment:
The City Accountant assured compliance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:
The CAO to ensure that all contributions are remitted in full to the PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG and GSIS within the prescribed period and to look into the details of employees from whom said deductions were made to facilitate the remittance of P1,147,395.74 to the Pag-IBIG; and
The City Personnel Office to expedite the reconciliation of their records with the GSIS Service Records to come up with the updated service records so that the full remittance of contributions to the GSIS could be undertaken by the CAO.
Management comment:
The City Accountant informed that their Office together with the City Personnel Office is coordinating with the PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG and GSIS in order to expedite the reconciliation of their records with said Offices so that billing and posting will be updated.  Once properly billed, full remittance will be made.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management enforce immediate settlement of the audit disallowances.
Management comment:

             accounts;


           •  Adjust  the  affected   accounts,   as   a   result   of   the
             reconciliation, that are determined to be  under/overstated  to
             present the  correct  balances  of  PPE  in  in  the  financial
             statements  and   effect   adjustments   of   the   accumulated
             depreciation, if necessary; and

       b. The GSO immediately provide copies of the AIR,  Invoice,  Purchase
          Order and Purchase Request to the CAO to facilitate the  immediate
          recording of the asset and Accounts Payable.

We recommended that the CAO record back  to  the  books  the
       Infrastructure Assets that were previously transferred  to  the  RPI
       before CY 2016 by using the appropriate accounts prescribed  in  the
       Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs and compute for the  depreciation
       expenses and accumulated depreciation of those assets.   Henceforth,
       recognize in the books the Infrastructure Assets and provide for the
       depreciation and accumulated depreciation.

We recommended that:

       a. The Inventory Committee conduct a  physical  count  for  the  Road
          Networks and prepare the RPCRNS  to  establish  the  validity  and
          existence of the account balance in the financial statements; and

       b. The General Services  Office  maintain  the  required  records  to
          facilitate the conduct of inventory and preparation of the RPCRNS.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:


       a. The City Engineering Office to -


           • Issue certificate of completion  for  projects  implemented  by
             administration in the accumulated amount of P3,288,431.56;


           • Expedite the processing of claims from supplier  to  facilitate
             the recording in the appropriate asset accounts  in  the  books
             the completed projects by contract totaling P51,615,505.69,  or
             if the contractor  is  no  longer  interested  to  collect  the
             balances of the contracts, consider said  balances  as  savings
             and issue  the  corresponding  certificate  of  completion  and
             acceptance;

       b. The CAO to -


           • Coordinate with the City Engineering Office to verify  projects
             already completed with accumulated cost of  P3,288,431.56,  and
             prepare the adjusting entries to close the CIP Accounts to  the
             proper asset account and record the corresponding depreciation;


           • Record completed projects identified as dormant for  more  than
             two to nine years with a total  cost  of  P30,096,648.56  after
             securing the documents from the City Engineering Office;


           • Adjust the amount of P43,635.00  recorded  as  Construction  in
             Progress instead of Repairs and Maintenance; and


           • Reclassify the unissued/unused materials directly  recorded  as
             CIP totaling P333,131.00 to  Construction  Materials  Inventory
             account.

We recommended that:

       a. The CAO prepare a  journal  entry  to  record  back  the  Terminal
          Benefits Payable;


       b. The CAO and the City Budget Office prepare and submit  a  list  of
          unclaimed Terminal Benefits to the City Personnel Office (CPO) for
          proper action; and

       c. The CPO apprise the employees of their  unclaimed  TLB,  upon  the
          receipt of the list of unclaimed Terminal Benefits.

We recommended that the City charge food and drinks expenses relative
      to  hosting  of  various  meetings,  assemblies,   sessions,   public
      relations, seminars and medical missions to Representation  Allowance
      (RA)/RE/EME accounts instead to OMOOE.

We recommended that Management:


       a. Specify the agenda during each meeting;



       b. Support payments for food and  drinks  with  minutes  of  meetings
          conducted specifying the date, time  and  agenda;  pictures  taken
          during the activities; and other documents to prove occurrence  of
          the activity; and


       c. Minimize the hosting of meetings which are not (a)  supportive  of
          the mandate/objectives of the City Government, (b) dictated by the
          demands of good government and  (c)  essential  or  could  not  be
          dispensed with, would not result to loss or damage  to  government
          property or disrupt services/operations.

We recommended that Management:

       a. Seek another eligible supplier that could offer lower prices; and



       b. Post the RFQs in the PhilGEPS website to enable wide coverage  and
          competition that gives most advantageous price and wide  selection
          pursuant to Section 54.2 of IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended and the management concurred that the City:

       a. Appropriate under  the  General  Fund  the  amortization  for  the
          payment of loans of the projects not eligible  for  funding  under
          the 20% Development Fund; and


       b. Utilize the Special Education Fund for the construction of  school
          buildings and facilities such  as  gymnasium  and  covered  courts
          which are among the authorized uses of the fund under  Item  4.1.2
          of DepEd/DBM/DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017.

We recommended that the Executive Office:


       a. Establish specific guidelines on the grant of  medical  assistance
          in order to set consistency and equality in the  release  thereof;
          and
       b. Seek the assistance  of  the  City  Health  Office  (CHO)  on  the
          determination of the illness, medical procedures and the cost  for
          treatment.  The determined amount that corresponds to each  nature
          of assistance must be strictly enforced to ensure  uniformity  and
          consistency in the grant of assistance.

We recommended that the CSWDO:


       a. Require the submission of required documents before the release of
          medical assistance; and


       b. Seek the assistance of the CHO on the identification  of  standard
          documents for each request for assistance.

We commend the Gender and Development (GAD) Focal  Point  System  of
       the City for consistently complying with the submission to the DILG,
       a GPB that  includes  gender  responsive  PPAs.  However,  we  still
       recommended  for  the  implementation  of   the   “Update   of   the
       comprehensive sex-disaggregated data  output  from  Community  Based
       Monitoring System” in CY 2019 to ensure that the database is updated
       for gender-responsive planning, programming and  policy  information
       as required under Section 37 (D), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9710.

We recommended that the GAD Focal  Point  System  Technical  Working
       Group ensure the timely submission of the GPB so that the DILG could
       release the reviewed GPB in accordance with the schedule prescribed
       under Item 4 of the same JMC and the City Planning  and  Development
       Office to include the endorsed  GPB  PPAs  in  the  AIP  within  the
       required period before the budget preparation.

We commended the City for meeting  its  over-all  projected  income,
       however, we still recommended that:

       a. The Local Finance Committee provide estimates  for  revenue  items
          with actual receipts but no projections;


       b. The concerned revenue generating offices –




           • Perform periodic review to ascertain  the  realization  of  the
             over-all estimated income; and


           • Analyze the reasons for revenue items that  missed  the  target
             and undertake effort to improve collection efficiency.

We recommended that the City adopt strategies, such as the  issuance
       of warrant of levy and  the  institution  of  civil  action  against
       delinquent  real  property  taxpayers  to  improve  the   collection
       efficiency.   Since the barangays are receiving  their  shares  from
       the realty tax collection of  the  city,  their  assistance  may  be
       sought in the implementation of the  city’s  tax  campaign  program,
       particularly in the dissemination  of  notices  of  delinquency  and
       serving of warrants of levy to the delinquent real  property  owners
       within their territorial jurisdiction.

We recommended that the City Mayor  require  the  City  Assessor  to
       expedite the preparation of  an  updated  SFMVs  as  basis  for  the
       conduct of a general revision  of  real  property  assessments,  and
       cause the drafting of an appropriate ordinance for consideration  of
       the Sanggunian Panlungsod.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Office  of  the  Mayor
       and General Services Office (GSO) require the supplier to  sign  the
       PO, specify the date when it is signed, indicate the stipulated date
       of delivery and brand name, to protect the interest of the City.

We recommended and Management concurred that the GSO submit  Notices
       of  Deliveries  within  24  hours  from  acceptance  of  deliveries;
       otherwise, non-compliance thereof warrants the issuance  of  Notices
       of Suspension.

We recommended that:

       a. The City stop the practice of splitting purchase  requisitions  or
          purchase orders and conduct competitive bidding to ensure that the
          City obtain the price that is most advantageous to the government;
          and


       b.  The  General  Services  Office  regularly  prepare   and   submit
          reasonable  estimates  for  the  materials  to  be  used  for  the
          immediately succeeding Calendar Year (or semester or  quarter)  in
          the  repair  and  maintenance  of  vehicles,  for  budgeting   and
          procurement purposes.

We recommended that the City,  through  the  Office  of  the  Mayor,
       facilitate the creation of the IASO as per City  Ordinance  No.  018
       series of 2016, and adopt the Internal  Audit  Manual  for  LGUs  as
       guide to the operation of the IAS.

We recommended that:


        a. The City Personnel Office expedite the reconciliation  of  their
           records with the GSIS  Service  Records  to  come  up  with  the
           updated service records so that full remittance of contributions
           could be undertaken by the City Accounting Office; and


        b. The City Accounting Office ensure that all taxes  withheld,  and
           contributions are remitted in full to the BIR, PhilHealth and
           Pag-IBIG within the deadline set.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:


       a. Follow-up with the Office of  the  Commissioner  and  LGS-NCR  the
          status of the petition and appeals on the NDs; and


       b. Exhaust all available remedies provided under the laws, rules  and
          regulations   in   enforcing   immediate   compliance   for    the
          refund/settlement for ND without pending appeal to COA as provided
          in the RRSA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:


       a. Strictly monitor the liquidation of cash advances (CAs) by sending
          demand letters regularly,  particularly  to  Accountable  Officers
          (AOs) who are active  in  service,  and  in  case  of  failure  to
          liquidate the same despite such demand, withhold the amount of any
          money due them pursuant to afore-cited Circular;


       b. Require the AOs to liquidate on time the cash advances pursuant to
          Item 5 of COA Circular 97-002 and Section 89 of PD 1445;


       c. Refrain from granting additional cash advance unless the  previous
          ones  are  already  liquidated,  pursuant   to   the   afore-quoted
          provisions to safeguard the fund from misuse or  any  events  that
          are beyond the control of the AO; and


       d. Schedule the distribution, if multiple CAs are inevitable such  as
          financial assistance and birthday cash gift, to give time for  the
          AO to organize  and  submit  liquidation  reports  and  supporting
          documents before the additional CAs are undertaken.

We recommended that:


          a. The City Accountant record only Other  Supplies  and  Materials
             Expense upon issuance of other supplies and  materials  to  the
             end-users; and


          b. The General Services Office include in the Report  of  Physical
             Count of Inventory all supplies and materials in the  warehouse
             as of year-end.

We recommended that the City Accountant:


       a. Gather all supporting documents to  record  all  assets  mentioned
          which were found to be included in the RPCPPE as  City’s  property
          but not recorded in the books of accounts; and


       b. Immediately look into the noted discrepancies  and  determine  the
          factors that contributed to the total variance of P373.027 million
          and effect/prepare the necessary adjusting entries as the case may
          be.

We recommended that:


       a.   The City General Services  Officer  submit  all  the  remaining
          Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) to the City Accountant especially
          for those projects that were already completed;


       b.   The City Engineer issue certificate of completion for  projects
          implemented by administration; and


       c.   The City Accountant prepare the adjusting entries to record the
          issuance   of   inventories   for    projects    implemented    by
          administration,  close  the  CIP  accounts  to  the  proper  asset
          accounts  upon  completion  of  the  projects   and   record   the
          corresponding depreciation.

We recommended that the City Accountant recognize  the  Accounts
       Payable and the corresponding expense accounts only upon delivery of
       goods/services pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated  December
       1, 2015.

We recommended that the City Accountant:


       a. Verify the said unpaid obligations if supported with valid claims,
          otherwise, revert back the amount of P3,310,520.44 to the  General
          Fund to be used for additional source of supplemental budget; and



       b. Record only claims duly supported  with  Sales  Invoice,  Delivery
          Receipt, Inspection and Acceptance Reports and Billing  Statements
          or Statement of Accounts.

We recommended that the City  Accountant  review  the  financial
       records and reports to ensure that all  financial  transactions  are
       taken up during the year and reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended the following:

       a. The Executive and  Legislative  Offices  assign  the  City  Social
          Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) to  monitor  the  grant  of
          educational assistance to prevent from duplication  and  exceeding
          the limitation set forth by the City’s internal  policy  under  EO
          No. 02, Series of 2018, issued by the Office of  the  City  Mayor.
          The amount of P50,000.00 per year is  material  for  the  indigent
          individuals, hence, more individuals will be enticed to avail  the
          same, as such,  centralized  monitoring  should  be  installed  to
          prevent the occurrence of the afore-cited observation;  and


       b.  The  City  Council  being  the  legislative  body  of  the  Local
          Government Unit, and  whose  duties  and  functions  is  to  enact
          ordinance, approve  resolutions  and  appropriate  funds  for  the
          general welfare of the City and its inhabitants (Section 458 of RA
          7160) should not be involved in the granting and  distribution  of
          financial assistance which is  an  executive  function  and  which
          should be best undertaken by the CSWDO.

We recommended that Management:


       a.    Stop paying Transportation Allowance (TA)  to  officials  with
          issued government vehicles pursuant to Item 8.2.1 of the DBM Local
          Budget Circular No. 103; and



       b. Require the refund of the paid TA for CY 2017 in the total  amount
          of P574,792.00.

We recommended that the City Human Resource Officer specify  the
       consultants’ duties/functions in  the  contracts  as  bases  in  the
       enforcement of their deliverables with the end  view  of  maximizing
       the worth of hiring said consultants and minimizing monetary  losses
       to the City Government.

We recommended that the City Human Resource Officer revisit  the
        qualifications,  duties/functions  of  the  existing  manpower   and
        service requirements to assess  the  actual  number  of  consultants
        needed and those that can be undertaken by the existing office.

We recommended that the City Human Resource Officer require  the
       submission of accomplishment reports/proof of actual performance and
       attach the same to the payroll as basis for payment.

We recommended that the City Accountant:


       a.   Request the depository banks  to  submit  the  bank  statements
          including debit/credit memos in (DM/CM), paid checks,  etc.  on  a
          monthly basis and continuous follow-up should be  made  after  the
          end of each month;  and


       b.   Henceforth, submit  for  audit  the  BRS  on  a  regular  basis
          pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

We recommended that the City ensure the provision of P28,639,583.35 in
       the supplemental budget in CY 2018 or annual/supplemental budget in
       CY 2019 for the refund from the GF to the SEF or in case the GF has
       no enough fund to reimburse the same, the City should set a
       timeframe to refund said amount.

We recommended that:


       a. The City Government include in the CY 2018 supplemental budget  or
          in CY 2019 Annual/Supplemental Budget  in  the  General  Fund  the
          refund to SEF the amount of P100,459,988.60;  and


       b.   The City School Board restrict the allocation  and  utilization
          of the SEF to the programs and projects specifically defined under
          the afore-stated law, rules and regulations in order  to  maximize
          the benefits that the  public  elementary  and  secondary  schools
          could derive from this fund.

We recommended that the City ensure   the   provision   of
       P29,835,577.25  in  the  supplemental   budget   in   CY   2018   or
       annual/supplemental budget in  CY  2019  for  the  refund  from  the
       General Fund to the Trust Liability-DRRM  account  or  in  case  the
       General Fund has no enough fund to  reimburse  the  same,  the  City
       should set a timeframe to refund the balance of P29,835,577.25.

We recommended that the City Engineer post tarpaulins  specially
       on infrastructure projects, with  the  required  specifications  and
       complete  information  to   fully   inform   the   public   of   the
       Programs/Projects and Activities of the City.


   We also recommended that the City Engineer submit  justification
       on their failure to comply with COA Circular 2013-004.

We recommended that the City adopt the 5th  Edition  Philippine
       Bidding Documents and the prescribed forms  to  clearly  define  the
       duties, obligations  and  responsibilities  of  the  contractor,  to
       ensure  the  proper  and  timely  implementation  of  infrastructure
       projects and protect the interest of the City.

We recommended that the BAC and the  members  of  the  Technical
       Working  group  should  carefully   evaluate   the   technical   and
       eligibility documents submitted to ensure that contracts are awarded
       to eligible bidders;

We recommended that the:


       a.   BAC require the winning bidders to  post  Performance  Security
          whose coverage include the defects liability period, which is from
          start of the contract until the final acceptance (one  year  after
          project completion);  and


       b.   BAC  and  the  City  Engineer  require   the  contractors  with
          extended  project  implementation  or  those   whose   Performance
          Security have already expired but the projects are still on-going,
          to  extend  the  validity  period  or  post  another   Performance
          Security.

We recommended the following:


       a. The City pay the mobilization only when it is ready  to  implement
          the project to preclude substantial amount of money in  the  hands
          of the contractor without any work being done;  and



       b. The City Engineering Office  undertake  all  detailed  engineering
          activities and iron out problems  before  the  conduct  of  public
          bidding to avoid delay in project implementation.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that  the  GFPS
       TWG ensure the timely submission of the GPB so that the  DILG  would
       be able to release the reviewed GPB in accordance with the  schedule
       prescribed under 4.1.C.5 (Schedule to be observed  in  GAD  Planning
       and Budgeting) of the same JMC.

we recommended that the City Council act
       positively on the recommendation of the MGFPS to delete  Section  V,
       Article II, Chapter III of Ordinance No. 036 Series of 2015  or  the
       GAD Code for being inconsistent with Annex D of JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the CSWDO:

       a.   Clearly identify the obligations from  LCPC  fund  and  general
          fund upon submission to the City  Budget  Office  for  the  proper
          charging of expenditures; and


       b.   Implement planned  PPAs  from  the  prepared  Annual  Work  and
          Financial Plan and maximize the utilization of the allocated  fund
          for the betterment of children’s general welfare and protection.

We recommended that Management:


       a. Follow-up with the Office of the  Commissioner  and  LGS-NCR  the
          status of the petition and appeals on the NDs; and


       b.    Exhaust all available remedies provided under the laws,  rules
          and  regulations  in  enforcing  immediate  compliance   for   the
          refund/settlement for ND without pending appeal to COA as provided
          in RRSA.

We recommended that the CPO expedite the reconciliation of their records with the GSIS Service Records to come up with the updated service records and thereby full remittance could be undertaken by the CAO.

We also reiterated that GSIS contributions once withheld should be automatic-ally remitted to the GSIS.

observations.recommendation:
1.8	We recommended that the Acting City Treasurer deposit intact daily or not later than the next banking day all collections and collections turned over to him pursuant to Section 8 and 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and Section 32, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs.
1.9	The City Accountant commented that the amount of P3,149,062.01 out of P3,390,143.42 was check collections for the fund transfer of BIR remittances.  The amount of P241,081.41 are cash collections, the actual receipts thereof occurred after December 31, 2016 but recorded as collections as of December 31, 2016, because they pertain to CY 2016 transactions and posting of the same were made before the closing of the books of accounts.  The City Accountant also commented that they will abide by the audit recommendation for CY 2017 collections so that cash on hand as of year-end will reflect the actual cash on hand in the treasury.

3.9	We recommended that management:
Cause or order the withholding of the payment of any money due the Officers who were recipient of the Letter of Demand dated October 17, 2016, pursuant to Item 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
Instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to strictly monitor the liquidation of cash advances by sending demand letters regularly, particularly to accountable officers/employees who are still in active service, and in case of failure to liquidate the same despite such demand, withhold the amount of any money due them pursuant to aforecited circular;  and
Refrain from granting another cash advance to officers and employees with unliquidated or unsettled cash advance.
3.10	The City Accountant informed that they had already sent notice and demand to officers/employees with unliquidated cash advances.  As a result, some personnel had liquidated their cash advances totaling P92,000.00, while others have yet to comply with the directive to liquidate their long outstanding cash advances.

4.4	We recommended that the CAO exert more efforts to locate the borrowers who were residents of the City, with remaining balances in the livelihood loans assistance they availed through the mentioned program by sending collection letters, among others, to the said borrowers, and/or referring the matter to the City Legal Office for the demand of payment of the said long outstanding loans receivable.

5.13	We recommended the following:
a.	The Inventory Committee to:
Reconcile all discrepancies between physical and book inventories.  Said discrepancies must be investigated, cleared, and reconciled immediately and that written explanation shall be required from accountable officer and responsible person(s) so that appropriate action may be undertaken to deter similar deficiencies in the future;
Coordinate with the City Accounting Office (CAO) for the correct quantity and unit costs of inventory items under Office Supplies Inventory account;  and
Include stock/code numbers for every item in the RPCI of Drugs and Medicine Inventory account to facilitate the reconciliation of inventory items.
b.	The CAO to:
Provide unit costs for all inventory items with undetermined variance for construction materials;
Provide unit cost for drugs and medicine inventory included in the physical count but not recorded in the books and effect necessary adjustments in the books;
Provide details on the acquisition dates of medicines to monitor its shelf life;  and
Make necessary adjustments to the inventory accounts based on the existing quantities at year-end.
5.14	The CGSO informed that they will undertake the following:
To have a uniform stock/code number and item description, and to adopt the stock/code number and item description used by the Accounting Office;
Assign a stock code number for drugs and medicines and the same shall be adopted by CHO in its  inventory and by the CGSO in its RPCI;  and
Furnish the CHO with information for the inclusion of the complete item description and date of acquisition of the drugs and medicines.
5.15	The City Accountant commented that the process of reconciling the variance between the report of physical count and books of accounts of Inventories account is ongoing.  As compliance with the recommendations, they have provided significant details such as the unit cost of the 32 inventory items under Construction Materials Inventory account, the correct quantities and unit cost of the Office Supplies Inventory account.  They also included the stock/code numbers and details on acquisition to monitor shelf life of the medicines and assured that all necessary adjustments shall be made as a result of reconciliation of the records.
7.9	We recommended that the CAO determine the cost of depreciable road network components and recognize their corresponding allowance for depreciation in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

8.14	We recommended that the City Government through the MCTCAO:
a.	Send statements of account to the lessee for all the months in arrears including penalties and surcharge;  and
b.	Refer the collection of the arrears to the City Legal Office for the issuance of final demand letter to the defaulting lessee to settle the unpaid monthly rentals in the amount of P578,985.00 (excluding penalties and surcharge) as of December 31, 2016.
8.15	We also recommended that the City Legal Office effect the necessary sanction in accordance with contract of lease or any legal remedies deemed necessary to effect the payment of the long overdue rental fees for the benefit of the City Government.
8.16	The Management, thru the Officer-in-Charge, Cultural Affairs Office, commented that they have endorsed the Statement of Balance Account of KnKFCC from September 2014 to April 2017 amounting to P638,880.00 to the City Legal Office dated May 3, 2017.
8.17	The City Legal Office explained that, to date, the lessee paid P99,000.00 and they will pursue collections of the remaining balance.  In case of failure, they will exhaust legal remedies and/or file a collection case accordingly.

10.19	We recommended the following:
For the City Relations Officer to:
Require the NGOs/POs who were recipient of the fund transfers/ financial assistance totaling P4,190,200.00 to submit: (i)  final Fund Utilization Report certified by their Accountant and approved by their  President/Chairman together with the inspection report and certificate of project completion rendered/issued by the City’s authorized representative; (ii) list of beneficiaries with their acceptance/acknowledgement of the project/funds/goods/services received;  and (iii) return of any amount not utilized to complete the project, if any.  These documents shall support the liquidation of funds granted to the said NGOs/POs;
For the City Accounting Office to:
Adjust the Donations account and Due from NGOs/POs in the amount corresponding to the fund assistance granted to NGOs/POs and furnish the Audit Team a copy of the journal entry voucher pertaining to said adjustments;
Require the submission of the liquidation report on the released funds by the implementing NGOs/POs and record the liquidation in the books of accounts;  and
Henceforth, require completion of the documentary requirements enumerated under “requisites for entitlement to government funds” as per Item No. 4.4 of COA Circular No. 2017-001 and record fund releases to NGOs/POs as Due from NGOs/POs, and the corresponding expense account upon liquidation of the said advances.
10.20	The Acting Chief, Community Relations Office (CRO) commented that they will comply with the recommendations and requested to give them ample time to contact the organizations/associations to require them to submit the fund utilization report.

11.10	We recommended that the City Social Welfare and Development Office:
Provide a centralize record that contains list of indigent individuals, organizations and representatives of various organizations and/or device a program (e.g. MS Excel) that will facilitate monitoring of availments to avoid duplication of benefits received by the indigent-beneficiary, and to be cautious that no entitlements-benefits will be received more than the limitations set forth in the City’s internal policy as embodied in EO No.  021 s.  of 2010;  and
Give financial assistance directly to the indigent beneficiary to ensure the actual receipt of the amount granted, or impose stricter control measures in the release of benefits/assistance to the representative of the organizations.
11.11	During the exit conference, the Management informed that they will issue an updated and harmonized executive order that pertains to fund releases to NGOs/POs and donations to indigent individuals to addressed the lapses in the internal control in granting the financial assistance.

12.6	We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation for the CGM to include in the CY 2017 supplemental budget or in CY 2018 annual/supplemental budget of the General Fund the refund to the SEF of the amount of P32,139,583.35.
12.7	The City Budget Officer commented that they are committed to refund said amount whenever funds are available.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
12.9	The Management should prioritize the said refund since said amount was utilized to PPAs that were not compliant with law, rules and regulations.  They should set a timeline when to refund the same amount so that the SEF will not be continuously deprived of the funds to be utilized for its compliant PPAs.

13.8	We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the City School Board:
Refrain from utilizing the SEF for the payment of expenses other than those enumerated as allowed under Sections 272 and 100 of RA 7160 and the appropriate issuances of DECS, DBM  and DILG;  and
Henceforth, restrict the allocation and prioritization of the SEF to the programs and projects specifically defined under the afore-stated law, rules and regulations in order to maximize the benefits that the public elementary and secondary schools could derive from this fund.

14.7	We recommended that the City School Board ensure the timely submission and approval of resolution for supplemental budget of the SEF already available so that the implementation of identified projects to be charged against the said budget could be undertaken and the equipment and facilities needed by the students in the public schools are delivered without delay.

15.16	We recommended that Management maintain a separate SL for each package per health facility/health center per allocation in order to establish a correct data for the receipts, utilization and balances for each package per allocation of every health facility and immediately implement the purpose for which the fund was created in compliance with the provisions of various PhilHealth Circulars and City Ordinance No. 037, Series of 2015.

16.5	We recommended and the Management concurred to utilize the remaining 40 percent operating costs pertaining to drugs and medicines of the PhilHealth capitation of PCB-1 for the purchase of primary care medicines listed in the Philippine National Drug Formulary to augment its drugs and medicines requirements at the HCs in addition to the 40 percent of the fund for equipment, supplies, training expenses and repair of medical equipment.

17.7	We recommended that the City Management through the City Health Office utilize the 20 percent, 40 percent and 30 percent of the PhilHealth capitation for PCB-1 package, MCP and NCP, respectively, for the honoraria of physicians and health/non-health staffs and to distribute regularly said honoraria for the benefits of the City health personnel concerned.
17.8	The City Health Officer commented that payment of honoraria for health and non-health personnel were given last December 2016, which covers the period of December 2015 to November 2016.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
17.9	Based on the Subsidiary Ledgers only the honoraria for the providers of TB DOTS and ABT packages were paid and no record of payments for the honoraria of the health providers of PCB-1, MCP and NCP.

18.5	We recommended that the City Government look into the ending inventory of slow moving medicines and prioritize the dispensation of the same.  Henceforth, reduce the quantity of slow moving medicines to be procured in the ensuing year so that the savings for the said reduction will be used to augment the requirement of the fast moving medicines or medicines needed according to the prevailing social health condition in the area.
18.6	The City Health Officer commented that they will ensure the process of procuring and dispensing more on fast moving drugs and medicines.  As regards to other concerns on expiring medicines allocated to health centers, the same were collectively retrieved and dispensed and utilized at once for medical clinic patients and clientele.

19.13	We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Propose allocation of the LDRMF based on PPAs that are integrated in an approved AIP and utilize the fund only on PPAs that are included therein pursuant to the afore-cited rules and regulations.  If the PPAs are not included in the AIP or included therein but not consistent with the LDRRMP, then the AIP must be revised in accordance with the LDRRMP to ensure that the PPAs are well coordinated with the other PPAs of the City and the plan-budget linkage are in place;  and
Henceforth, strictly monitor and evaluate the use and disbursement of the LDRRMF based on the LDRRMP as incorporated in the local development plan and annual work and financial plan as mandated in Section 21 of RA 10121.

20.11	We recommended that the City Government:
Consider the provision of P13,689,783.00 in the CY 2017 supplemental budget or in CY 2018 annual/supplemental budget in the General Fund to refund the Trust Liability-DRRM account with the same amount;  and
Henceforth, refrain from charging against the LDRRMF projects and activities that are not included or related to the DRRM activities as declared under Section 21 of RA 10121 and Section 3.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.  Be guided with the provisions of Section 5.0 of NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG JMC No.  2013-1 as to the appropriate utilization of the LDRRMF, specifically with regard to the projects and activities for each thematic areas of disaster management enumerated therein.
20.12	The Management requested that the disbursement for the CCTV cameras be given consideration since they were procured for the monitoring of flood in the flood prone areas such as roads that are near to the creek and Marikina River.  The constituents are also monitoring the flood level through the City’s monitoring system.  Such monitoring system is operating with the help of CCTV cameras.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
20.13	The Audit Group required the Management to submit data or map containing streets that are near the creek and Marikina River with installed CCTV cameras.  Other disbursements are still not allowed to be charged against the LDRRMF.

21.4	We recommended that the City Government of Marikina include the amount of P19,645,794.25 in the CY 2018 annual/supplemental budget for the refund from the General Fund to the Trust Liability-DRRM account.
21.5	The City Budget Officer commented that they are committed to refund said amount whenever funds are available.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
21.6	The Management should prioritize the refund since said utilization was not compliant with law, rules and regulations.  They should set a timeline when to refund to the Trust Liability-DRRM account.
21.7	During the exit conference, it was emphasized that all ineligible expenses cannot be allowed and reimbursement from the General Fund must be prioritized.

22.9	We recommended that the Management:
Strictly impose the warranty in the procurement of goods which is covered by retention money in an amount equivalent to at least one percent (1%) of every progress payment or a special bank guarantee of at least one percent (1%) of the total contract price effective October 29, 2016 as amended under the  2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184;
Impose the warranty security in accordance with the schedule and forms prescribed under Section 62.2.3.3 of the IRR of RA 9184 for the procurement of infrastructure projects to protect the City’s interest against structural defects;  and
Require the contractor of the newly built PLMAR School Building and infrastructure projects enumerated in Annex Q hereof, to submit the warranty security as mandated under Section 62 of RA 9184 and Section 62.2.3.3 of its IRR, which should have been included in the DVs pertaining to the payments of the mentioned infrastructure projects.  The mentioned transactions with incomplete documentary requirements such as warranty security will be suspended in audit.

23.8	We recommended that:
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) thru its Secretariat:
Submit copies of (i) Minutes of bid opening and preliminary evaluation of bids, (ii) Bid Evaluation Report, and (iii) Post Qualification and Evaluation Report;  and
Comply with the minimum mandatory requirements enumerated in Item 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001, particularly on supporting the disbursement voucher with documents that emanate from the BAC such as the minutes of bid opening and preliminary evaluation of bids, Bid Evaluation Report and Post Qualification and Evaluation Report.
The City Accountant:
Ensure completeness of required supporting documents to the disbursement voucher prior to its approval as prescribed in Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012.

24.4	We recommended that the MGFPS continuously and consistently adopt the GAD Code as basis in the identification of issues/mandate and PPAs on GAD and ensure the proper implementation of all identified PPAs during the year.
25.7	We recommended that Management ensure the timely submission of the GPB so that the DILG would be able to release the reviewed GPB in accordance with the schedule prescribed.

26.6	We recommended and the Management agreed to propose to the City Council the amendment of Section V, Article II, Chapter III of Ordinance No. 036 Series of 2015 or the GAD Code, which provides the grant of honoraria to MGFPS for being contrary to existing rules and regulations particularly the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

27.8	We recommended that Management:
Require the Chairmen of the Committee on Women and Family Affairs and Committee on Education and the Officer-In-Charge, City Social Welfare and Development Office to prepare the Council’s Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, and Local Investment Plant for Children as required by DILG MC No. 2012-120;  and
Allocate 1% of IRA in its annual budget for the Marikina City Council for the Protection of Children (MCCPC) pursuant to R.A. No. 9344 and City Ordinance No. 07-098 to ensure that all plans for children are provided with funds and properly implemented.

27.11	Although the programs/projects/activities funded by the amount of P1,999,750.00 are considered PPAs as cited in DILG MC No. 2012-120, there was no statement that such amount was charged against the 1% of IRA for LCPC and such PPAs were implemented in accordance with the Council’s Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, and Local Investment Plant for Children.
27.12	Notwithstanding, the LCPC is required to prepare the said plan pursuant to DILG MC No. 2012-120 and the City Budget Officer to allocate 1% of IRA in its annual budget for the Marikina City Council for the Protection of Children (MCCPC) pursuant to R.A.  No.9344 and City Ordinance No. 07-098.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The City refrain from transferring the funds for the SFP to the OL/identified partner of the program and administer faithfully such fund as provided for under Item VII.C.3 of the Omnibus Guidelines in the Implementation of SFP and as the implementing agency of the fund in accordance with COA Circular No.  94-013;  and

The CSWDO performs all tasks pertaining to the feeding operations such as food purchases, and submission of required physical and financial reports and liquidation of the fund and other duties as the focal person in the implementation of the program in accordance with DSWD AO No.  08 or the Omnibus Guidelines in the implementation of the SFP.

The City through the CSWDO:

Require the Day Care Workers to include in the Master List not only the names but also the addresses of the children beneficiaries in the DCC and SNP which is a vital data/information in the monitoring and follow-up mechanisms or programs concerning delivery of basic services;

Submit class photos/pictures as part of the documentation of the actual conduct of feeding program taken or captured on the actual dates printed on the said photographs;

Submit the Weight Monitoring Form, Daily Attendance Sheet and Master List of Beneficiaries from various DCC and SNP, where the names of the children are alphabetically arranged by surname as part of the database profiling and updating of children beneficiaries of the program as stated in item VII.C.3.16 of the Omnibus Guidelines;

Require the OL, District 2, City of Marikina to submit the Weight Monitoring Form, Daily Attendance Sheet, and Master List of Beneficiaries with addresses from various feeding centers not covered by the SFP of the CGM, where the names of the children are alphabetically arranged by surname to facilitate review/ evaluation;  and

Submit list/ schedule to support the official receipts submitted showing details of the specific food items purchased for the daily hot meals to facilitate monitoring/validation of compliance to the menu cycle recommended by the DSWD based on the FNRI food preparation.

To improve the effectiveness of the program, the CSWDO:

Coordinate with the DCC workers so that a MOA or written agreement with the parents for their counterpart in the SFP must be established to prevent any negative implication and disagreement with the parents of the children beneficiaries.  If written agreement with the parents would not be feasible, the City should provide budget augmentation to achieve the desired outcomes of the SFP as provided in Item VII.C.3.10 of the Omnibus Guidelines;  and

Ensure that sessions on parent effectiveness under Item 4 of the Omnibus Guidelines be completely undertaken by the parents in a manner or methodology that will improve/ enhance their knowledge, attitude, skills and practices on parenting to achieve the goal of early childhood care and development through proper nutrition in the community
observations.managementAction: It is the counter-part of the LGU to provide the fund for the additional expenses like mineral water, milk, and LPG gas.  But since the LGU has no budget for the said expenses, the DSWD-NCR and the DCC workers together with the parents agreed for the collection of P5.00 to serve as the parent’s counterpart.

observations.recommendation:
The Local Development Council:

Specify the PPAs to be included in the LDP/CDC Resolution pursuant to Article 410 of the IRR of RA 7160;  and

Complete the information required to be included in the template of AIP Summary Form provided under Annex A of the DILG, NEDA, DBM and DOF JMC No.  1, such as (a) specific PPAs funded from the 20% DF, (b) timeframes, (c) expected output in quantifiable terms and (d) estimated cost for each PPAs.

The City Development Council and the City Government:

Appropriate and disburse the 20% Development Fund (DF)  strictly for investment and capital expenditures only pursuant to Section 2.3 of DILG-DBM JMC No.  2011-1 and include projects of the same kind as those enumerated in Section 3 of the same JMC;

Refund the 20% DF in the amount of P6,665,018.56 from the General Fund-Proper, to be added as continuing appropriation of the 20% DF;

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management ensure that the pertinent provisions of the GAD Code are considered in the gender mainstreaming of the City’s policies, programs and projects and GAD planning and budgeting integrated in the regular activities of the City.

Management ensures that their GPB is submitted to the DILG for their review and approval in accordance with the schedules to be observed in GAD planning and budgeting under 4.C.5 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2013-01.

Also, management ensures that the reviewed and approved GPB is integrated in the Annual Investment Program (AIP).

The Local Chief Executive to direct all the members of the GAD Management Team to coordinate in the formulation and implementation of the identified GAD PPAs.  The effective implementation lies primarily on the cooperation and shared-responsibilities among the members of the Team.

Also, the members of the GAD Management Team to:
